---
title: "On Looking at a Still Life"
description: "What is the point of painting a lemon? More than you might think."
author: "Sophia Jaffer"
date: "2025-01-20"
categories:
  - Painting
  - Essays
---

## The genre's bad reputation

Still life has always had a slight problem of status. For centuries it ranked at the bottom of the academic hierarchy of painting — below history painting, portraiture, landscape, even genre scenes of everyday life. It was considered suitable for minor painters and minor collectors. The big subjects — gods, heroes, allegories — required history painting. Still life gave you fruit and dead birds.

This prejudice, I think, entirely misunderstands what the best still life painting is doing.

## What the lemon is doing

Consider Zurbarán's lemons, in the painting in the Norton Simon Museum: three lemons on a silver plate, arranged with a formality that is almost severe. A cup. A flower. Nothing else. The background is dark.

The lemons are painted with extraordinary care. You can see the texture of the peel — the way it catches and absorbs light differently across its surface. The painting is not, however, really about lemons. It is about attention. It is about what happens when you look at something ordinary with such concentration that it becomes strange.

## Slowness as subject

Still life is, among other things, a meditation on time. The objects in a still life are paused. They are not going anywhere. This is obviously literally true — they are objects — but the paintings make something of this stillness. The lemon on Zurbarán's silver plate has been there for four hundred years. It will be there, presumably, for several hundred more.

There is something consoling about this. The things of the world persist. The light that falls on them persists. Looking persists.
